coredata.dc:title: Performance enhancement of catechin-graphene quantum dot nanocomposites functionalized with carboxyl and doped/decorated with boron towards dye-sensitized solar cell applications: DFT and TD-DFT calculations
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Molecular Graphics and Modelling
coredata.dc:creator: Alsmani, Nuha
Al-Qurashi, Ohoud S.
Wazzan, Nuha
coredata.dc:description: A photosensitizer plays a vital role in adjusting the optical and electrochemical properties that affect the performance of dye-sensitized solar cells (DSSCs). Therefore, it should meet critical requirements for efficient operation of DSSCs. This study proposes catechin, a natural compound, as a photosensitizer and modifies its properties through hybridization with graphene quantum dots (GQDs). Density functional theory (DFT) and time-dependent DFT methods were used to investigate the geometrical, optical, and electronic properties. Twelve nanocomposites of catechin attached to carboxylated/uncarboxylated GQD were designed. The GQD was further doped with central/terminal boron atom or decorated with boron groups (organo-borane, borinic, and boronic groups). The available experimental data of parent catechin was used to validate the elected functional and basis set. Through hybridization, the energy gap of catechin was significantly narrowed by 50.66–61.48%. Therefore, its absorption was shifted from the UV to the visible region which matches the solar spectrum. Also, increasing the absorption intensity led to high light-harvesting efficiency close to unity that can increase current generation. The energy levels of designed dye nanocomposites are appropriately aligned with the conduction band and redox potential, indicating the feasibility of electron injection and regeneration. The observed properties confirm that the reported materials exhibit characteristics of interest thus they could be promising candidates for applications in DSSCs.

head.ce:title: Performance enhancement of catechin-graphene quantum dot nanocomposites functionalized with carboxyl and doped/decorated with boron towards dye-sensitized solar cell applications: DFT and TD-DFT calculations

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A photosensitizer plays a vital role in adjusting the optical and electrochemical properties that affect the performance of dye-sensitized solar cells (DSSCs). Therefore, it should meet critical requirements for efficient operation of DSSCs. This study proposes catechin, a natural compound, as a photosensitizer and modifies its properties through hybridization with graphene quantum dots (GQDs). Density functional theory (DFT) and time-dependent DFT methods were used to investigate the geometrical, optical, and electronic properties. Twelve nanocomposites of catechin attached to carboxylated/uncarboxylated GQD were designed. The GQD was further doped with central/terminal boron atom or decorated with boron groups (organo-borane, borinic, and boronic groups). The available experimental data of parent catechin was used to validate the elected functional and basis set. Through hybridization, the energy gap of catechin was significantly narrowed by 50.66–61.48%. Therefore, its absorption was shifted from the UV to the visible region which matches the solar spectrum. Also, increasing the absorption intensity led to high light-harvesting efficiency close to unity that can increase current generation. The energy levels of designed dye nanocomposites are appropriately aligned with the conduction band and redox potential, indicating the feasibility of electron injection and regeneration. The observed properties confirm that the reported materials exhibit characteristics of interest thus they could be promising candidates for applications in DSSCs.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Authors confirm that the data supporting this study's findings are available within the article and its supplementary data.

ce:section.ce:para: The daily consumption of energy threatens the available energy content which makes it critical to discover other renewable sources. Solar energy has the most extensive source which can be considered as a potential alternative to fossil fuels where photovoltaic solar cells absorb photons from solar radiation and convert them into electrical energy [1–4]. Third generation solar cells can be categorized into four types depending on the used technologies which are nanocrystal based, polymer based, dye-sensitized, and concentrated solar cells [5]. Those novel technologies have been proposed and used in order to improve solar cells' efficiency, however, short lifetime and stability under long-time operation are the main obstacles hindering commercialization [6]. Dye-sensitized solar cells (DSSCs) are considered among the most promising solar cells due to their low cost, straightforward synthesis of materials, and eco-friendly characteristics [7]. DSSCs are made of a semiconductor photoanode covered by a layer of photosensitizer/dye known as a working electrode, a counter electrode, and a redox electrolyte [1,2].

Natural dyes on a TiO2 photoanode have been a point of interest for several researchers recently [3,4,8]. Singh et al. fabricated a DSSC using three anthocyanin dyes extracted from Begonia malabarica Lam. (BM) leaves, Melastoma malabathricum (MM) fruit, and pomegranate (POM) fruit in ethanol without purification [3]. The obtained open-circuit voltage (VOC) of the dyes increased according to the following trend MM (380 mV) < POM (480 mV) < BM (500 mV). A similar trend was observed in short-circuit current density (JSC) values. Furthermore, both VOC and JSC enhanced the power conversion efficiency (η) in the same order as MM (0.76%) < POM (1.12%) < BM (1.76%) [3]. Trihutomo et al. used chlorophyll extracted from papaya leaves and filled the TiO2 cavities with different concentrations from 0% to 75% with an increment of 25% of clathrin protein in order to increase the electron transfer characteristic [4]. An increase in both VOC and clathrin protein concentration was observed in the order: 526 mV (0%) < 590 mV (25%) < 624 mV (50%) < 657 mV (75%). A similar trend was observed in the η which increased from 0.047% (0%) to 1.465% (75%) which indicated that clathrin protein enhanced the electron transfer process [4]. In the study by Güzel et al., dyes extracts from Berberis vulgaris fruit (FBV), Berberis vulgaris root (RBV), Phytolacca americana fruit (FPA), and Phytolacca americana stalk (SPA) were used without further modification [8]. The major dyes in each extract are anthocyanin in FBV, isoquinoline alkaloid in RBV, and betalain for both FPA and SPA. The extracts exhibited optical and electrochemical properties that were suitable to function as sensitizers in DSSCs. The FPA and SPA extracts exhibit broader absorption peaks which increased the light harvesting capacity. Better light-harvesting capacities contributes to enhanced DSSC performance by generating more JSC. Thus, FPA and SPA demonstrated higher JSC (8.57–9.08 mA cm−2) in comparison to FBV and RBV (5.47–7.46 mA cm−2). The VOC values also followed a similar trend. Improvement in both JSC and VOC led to the highest efficiency of 3.04% from SPA extract [8].

Despite the interesting characteristics and advantages of natural dyes, they suffer from low power conversion efficiencies [5]. Recent studies showed that improved light harvesting efficiency (LHE) of photosensitizers could be achieved through the introduction of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) [1,2,9–11]. Gao and co-workers developed nanocomposites of several natural dyes hybridized with GQD [2,9–11]. Six carbazole-carbazole dyes with different π-linkers were hybridized with GQD and studied at both B3LYP/6-31G (d,p) and TD-CAM-B3LYP/6-311 + G (d,p) in tetrahydrofuran (THF) solvent using polarizable continuum model (CPM) [10]. Nanocomposites of natural dyes with GQDs exhibited larger driving forces (−2.27 eV to −2.32 eV) than isolated dyes (−1.43 eV to −2.06 eV). The intensity of absorption peaks increased leading to higher LHE (0.995%–0.998%) than isolated carbazole-carbazole dyes (0.916%–0.971%). According to that, higher LHE would generate more JSC of nanocomposites than isolated dyes [10]. Tetraphenyl-porphyrin (TPP) dye is a suitable reported sensitizer in DSSCs [11]. It was hybridized with GQD and graphene oxide quantum dots (GOQD) with a different number of hydroxyl groups GOQD-(2OH – 22OH). The hybridized nanocomposites of TPP with GQD and GOQD were studied theoretically at B3LYP/6-31 + G(d) and TD-PCM-wB97XD/6-31 + G(d) in THF solvent. All designed nanocomposites gave excellent optical and charge separation properties. Among all nanocomposites, GOQD-(18OH)-TPP is the most promising sensitizer due to its high driving force (−2.44 eV), VOC (1.47 eV), and highest LHE (0.998%) [11]. A DSSC was fabricated using phenoxazine based dyes with benzene (POXB) and pyrrole (POXP) π-spacers hybridized with GQD [9]. Four different sizes of GQD were designed as GQD1(C54H18), GQD2(C66H20), GQD3(C80H22), and GQD4(C96H24). B3LYP/6–31 + G(d) and TD- CAM-B3LYP/6–31 + G(d) methods were used to investigate the optical and electronic properties of the eight designed nanocomposites. The nanocomposites demonstrated improved LHE (0.9855%–0.9997%) in comparison to isolated dyes (0.8963%–0.9175%). GQD2-POXB, GQD3-POXB, and GQD4-POXB exhibit an intermolecular charge transfer (ICT) character which reduces the electron-hole recombination rate. GQD4-POXB is the most promising nanocomposite according to its high LHE (0.9995%) and VOC (1.30 eV) [9]. In another study, GQD was hybridized with eight anthoxanthin dyes (Flavone, Chrysin, Apigenin, Luteolin, 3-Hydroxyflavone, Galangin, Kaempferol, and Quercetin) and studied at B3LYP/6-31G (d,p) and TD-B3LYP/6-31G (d,p) in THF solvent with CPM [2]. The resulting nanocomposites shifted the absorption maxima of isolated dyes (187.19–363.81 nm) to longer wavelengths (449.05–456.42 nm) with higher intensities up to 1.7432. As a result, LHE is improved from 0.6396% - 0.6396%–0.9593% - 0.9819%. After coupling with TiO2, only GQD-Flavone and GQD- Chrysin are appropriately aligned with the TiO2 CB making them the most two promising nanocomposites [2]. Saedi et al. added GQDs to red and green algae extracts containing phycobilin and chlorophyll dyes, respectively [1]. Photoluminescence analysis confirmed that the addition of GQD reduces the electron-hole recombination process. GQD-phycobilin and GQD-chlorophyll obtained higher VOC (0.73 V and 0.75 V) and JSC (2.26 mA cm−2 and 2.04 mA cm−2) when compared to isolated phycobilin and chlorophyll. As a result, the η increased to 0.94% (GQD-phycobilin) and 0.81% (GQD-chlorophyll); hence, GQDs can enhance the performance of dyes in DSSCs [1].

Catechin is a polyphenol compound that belongs to the flavonoid family [12,13]. It can be extracted from several natural sources such as fruit, vegetables, herbs, and algae. The most common source is green tea which is one of the most abundant herbs in the world. Catechin works as a good chemoprotective antioxidant that has several medical applications such as an anticancer. Also presents the potential for the reduction of the risk of cardiovascular diseases. Natural extracts vary in catechin content types with different structures [12,13]. All types of catechin have similar physicochemical properties. Since they are organic molecules, they can be solvated in organic solvents such as water, methanol, and ethanol. They have maximum absorbance in the range 269–280 nm [13]. Soheil et al. fabricated a DSSC using catechin extracted from Areca catechu fruit [14]. They evaluated several solvents and found that methanol provides maximum absorption at 444 nm. The Photoluminescence emission spectra showed broad peaks leading to a low probability of electron-hole recombination rate. The Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectra confirmed the presence of hydroxyl functional group that strengthens the binding with TiO2 surface thus increasing the electron transfer rate. Catechin obtained a JSC of 0.2 mAcm−2, a VOC of 0.539 V, and a fill factor (FF) of 72.53%. The observed low values of JSC and VOC are responsible for obtaining low conversion efficiency (η) value of 0.077% which needs to be enhanced [14].

One may observe that the improvement of the performance of most natural dyes in literature involved only hybridization with GQD and GOQD resulting in the reduction of energy gaps which in turn affects the absorbance by shifting to longer wavelengths with increased intensities. The consequence of increasing LHE leads to higher JSC values. These improvements in absorbance, JSC, and VOC achieved higher conversion efficiencies than isolated dyes.

Therefore, the present study focuses on the hybridization of catechin with uncarboxylated and carboxylated GQD to form nanocomposite materials (UG-C and CG-C). Also, as a first attempt, the study expanded to functionalize the UG-C and CG-C with boron atom/groups through doping or decorating UG-C and CG-C nanocomposites. The properties of the twelve designed nanocomposites were compared to catechin and compared with respect to each other. The calculated properties included binding energies, frontier molecular orbitals (FMOs), density of states (DOS), UV–Vis absorption spectra, emission properties, electrochemical parameters, reorganization energies, molecular description parameters, non-covalent interactions (NCI), intramolecular charge transfer (ICT), and non-linear optical (NLO) properties were calculated and discussed in detail. Hybridization of catechin with GQD tuned the energy gap that could shift the absorption of isolated catechin from the UV region to longer wavelengths in the visible region. This major improvement allows all catechin nanocomposites to function as suitable photosensitizers in DSSCs.

In this study, Gaussian 09 program was used to perform all the calculations [15]. Both GaussView 6.0.16 and VMD programs were used for visualization [16,17]. The geometries of all systems were optimized using density functional theory (DFT) calculations at Becke, 3-parameter, Lee–Yang–Parr (B3LYP) hybrid functional [18–21] with 6-31 + G (d,p) basis set [22,23]. Conductor-like Polarizable Continuum Model (CPCM) was used for simulating the methanol solvent [24,25].

UV–Vis absorption calculations were performed under the same level of theory. Nine functionals were evaluated on calculating the absorption of catechin. Those functionals differ in their Hartree-Fock exchange correlation percentage (HF%) which is in brackets. It was noted that lower HF% provides more accurate calculations. B3LYP (20%) [20] contains both local correlation (VWN) [21] and non-local correlation (LYP) [19]. B3P86 [18,26], and B3PW91 (20%) [18,27] contain non-local correlation provided by Perdew 86 and Perdew/Wang 91, respectively. Austin-Frisch-Petersson functional (APFD) includes the dispersion [28]. The long-range-Corrected Functionals CAM-B3LYP (19%–65%) [29] and wB97XD [30] (20%) give better results for long distances such as excitation process by including Coulomb-attenuating exchange and empirical dispersion, respectively. M06–2X (54%) [31,32] and M05–2X (56%) [33] the Truhlar Group functionals. mPW1PW91 [34] is a Becke One-Parameter Hybrid Functional using Perdew-Wang exchange with PW91 correlation. The mentioned nine functionals (B3LYP, B3P86, mPW1PW91, B3PW91, APFD, M06–2X, wB97XD, CAM-B3LYP, and M05–2X) gave maximum absorption of catechin as 210.96, 206.10, 204.58, 203.77, 202.57, 200.80, 198.95, 196.42, and 193.31 nm, respectively. The experimental maximum absorption wavelength of catechin is 278 nm [35,36]. All the calculated values are lower than the experimental value with small variations. The range of differences between the calculated results is only about 18 nm which is negligible, indicating that all functionals can effectively reproduce experimental observation. However, B3LYP hybrid functional showed the best matching result with the experimental data with the least percentage deviation equals 24.1% (Fig. 1 ). B3LYP functional was applied for the absorption calculations for all the systems that are under investigation.

Emission spectra were calculated by time-dependent DFT (TD-DFT) method at B3LYP hybrid functional with 6-31 + G (d,p) basis set in methanol solvent with the abovementioned solvation model. The emission spectrum is used to calculate the excited state lifetime (τ) by applying Eq. (1):

Non-covalent interactions (NCIs) were calculated using Multiwfn program [37]. These interactions are useful to understand the forces those affect the stability, and they give an important indication of the reaction process. Density of states (DOS) plots were calculated and plotted by GaussSum program [38]. These plots show the energy levels and the energy gaps in order to understand how hybridization affects them. Tuning the energy gaps is responsible about modifying several photo and electro parameters such as the absorption maxima.

Binding energy (E b) is used as an indication of the stability of the nanocomposite. As the E b increases, the stability increases. E b is calculated by subtracting the sum of atomic energies of carbon, hydrogen, oxygen, nitrogen, and boron (nE C, nE H, nE O, nE N, and nE B) from the total energy of the system (E system) and divided by the total number of atoms as shown in Eq. Open-circuit voltage (VOC) is the maximum voltage with no flowing current through the solar cell. Theoretically, it is based on the electron injection from the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) of the photosensitizer donor to the conduction band (CB) of the acceptor semiconductor TiO2 electrode. It is calculated by Eq. (5) where E CB is the energy of the TiO2 conduction band, E LUMO is the LUMO energy of the photosensitizer, and e is the electron charge (1 eV) [2,39,40].

The optimized structures of isolated catechin, (C), isolated graphene quantum dot (GQD), non-carboxylated nanocomposites, carboxylated nanocomposites, and the molecular electrostatic potential (MEP) maps of all nanocomposites are illustrated in Fig. 2 . In this study, uncarboxylated graphene quantum dot hybridized with catechin (UG-C) is the first modelled nanocomposite. The UG-C nanocomposite was carboxylated by two-edged carboxyl groups (CG-C). As can be seen in Fig. 2, carboxylation induces some polarity in nanocomposites lattice which enhances the electron transfer properties. Both UG-C and CG-C nanocomposites were doped and decorated with five different boron configurations. They are doping central substitutional boron (B1-UG-C), doping terminal boron at the edge (B2-UG-C), organo-borane group (B3-UG-C), borinic boron group (B4-UG-C), and boronic group (B5-UG-C).

Introducing boron to the GQD lattice gives p-type properties which increase the hole mobility of nanocomposites. Finally, for more enhancement, all nanocomposites were carboxylated to get the advantages of the high electronegativity of the carboxyl group and the p-type properties of the boron groups. The resulting carboxylated configurations are referred to as CG-C, B1-CG-C, B2-CG-C, B3-CG-C, B4-CG-C, and B5-CG-C.

Fig. 3 represents the binding energies of all designed nanocomposites. The calculated binding energies are positive which indicates the stability of all systems. Binding energies are ranging from 6.97 to 7.10 eV which means the influence of carboxylation and boron doping/decoration is negligible. Both carboxylated and in-plane doped systems (UG-C, CG-C, B1-UG-C, B1-CG-C, B2-UG-C, and B2-CG-C) have higher binding energies since the sp2 hybridization of the GQD lattice is not distorted. Boron in-plane doping is more stable at the edge rather than in the centre due to less induced strain as reported by Agnoli, S and Favaro, M [44]. Therefore, edge doping of B2-UG-C and B2-CG-C shows higher binding energies (7.049 and 7.092 eV respectively) than central doping of B1-UG-C and B1-CG-C (7.018 and 7.061 eV respectively). The higher stability of boron doping rather than boron decoration was reported in previous literature [44]. The decorated UG-C configurations have lower binding energies since the lattice of GQD is distorted unlike carboxylation which enhanced the stability of all systems since it increases the dipole moment and does not distort the GQD structure. This result matches the data from H. Abdelsalam et al. who proved higher stability due to carboxylation that increases with increasing the number of terminal carboxyl groups [45].

The frontier molecular orbitals (FMOs) of all photosensitizers and their energy level alignment with both the TiO2 conduction band (CB) and the
I −
/
I 3 −
redox potential (RP) are shown in Fig. 4 . There are two processes of electron transfer in DSSC, electron injection and dye regeneration. Fig. 4 indicates the spontaneity of both electron injection and regeneration processes. The excited free electrons are injected from the lowest unoccupied molecular orbitals (LUMOs) of the photosensitizers to the CB. Simultaneously, electrons are regenerated from the RP to the highest occupied molecular orbitals (HOMOs) of the photosensitizers in order to reduce the oxidized dye. Therefore, the LUMOs and the HOMOs of the nanocomposites should be less negative than the CB and more negative than the RP, respectively, to allow electrons to be transferred. As shown in Fig. 4, the LUMOs of all nanocomposites ranging from− 3.297 eV to −2.593 eV are less negative than the CB (4.3 eV) [40], this validates the electron injection process. The HOMOs of all nanocomposites ranging from −5.471 to −4.935 eV are more negative than the RP (−4.8 eV) [39]. This approves the ability of electron/dye regeneration of all systems.

As shown in Fig. 4, the LUMO of catechin has been stabilized by hybridization with GQD (UG-C). Further stabilization of UG-C was obtained by doping and decoration with boron. Also, carboxylation increased the stability of the LUMOs of all nanocomposites. As the distance (difference in energy) between the LUMOs of nanocomposites and the CB decreases, the easier the electrons can be injected into the CB since lower energy is needed to transfer an electron [46]. Energy loss can be avoided by stabilizing the energy levels which mismatches the LUMO and HOMO of the photosensitizer with the CB and the electrolyte, respectively [47]. Similarly, as the separation between the HOMOs of the nanocomposites and the RP decreases, the easier the electrons can be regenerated. As can be seen in Table SD 2, the LUMO of the catechin is 3.671 eV away from the CB. Whereas the energy difference between the LUMOs of the photosensitizers and the CB (E L-CB) of all nanocomposites does not exceed 1.7 eV. This obvious stabilization of the energy levels enhances the electron injection and prevents electron recombination. The energy difference between RP and the HOMOs of the photosensitizers (E RP-H) of catechin is 1.391 eV while the HOMOs of all nanocomposites are 0.67 eV below the RP as a maximum difference. This means the HOMOs of the nanocomposites are more suitable photosensitizers than isolated catechin for regenerating electrons from the RP.

Carboxylation stabilized both the LUMOs and HOMOs of all nanocomposites; hence, the LUMOs of carboxylated nanocomposites are better aligned with the CB while the HOMOs of uncarboxylated nanocomposites are better aligned with the RP. Fig. 4 illustrates that the LUMO of B5-CG-C has the most appropriate alignment with CB with only 1.003 eV difference. Unlike B2-UG-C which is 1.707 eV away from the CB. It is also found that B4-UG-C has the best HOMO alignment with the RP with a very low energy difference of 0.135 eV. On contrary, CG-C has the most stabilized HOMO with an energy difference of 0.671 eV from the RP. According to that, it is assumed that B5-CG-C and B4-UG-C are the best electron injectors and regenerators, respectively.

Narrower energy gaps require less energy to excite electrons from the ground state to the excited state; hence, more efficient light capturing can be obtained. As illustrated in Fig. 4, catechin has a large energy gap higher than 5 eV which gives it an insulation character. Combination of catechin with GQD decreased the energy gap by 50.66%. The huge reduction in the energy gap induced semiconducting character which enhances electron excitation and light capturing. The energy gap of UG-C (2.744 eV) decreased to 2.598–2.143 eV by doping and decoration with boron. B5-UG-C has the most reduction of catechin energy gap by 61.48%. Lower E g values were obtained by carboxylation in general. CG-C decreased the E g to 2.661 eV while carboxylating the doped and decorated systems decreased the E g to the range of 2.493–2.165 eV. It can be noticed that carboxylating the last boron configuration (B5-CG-C) led to a slight increase in the energy gap from 2.143 eV to 2.165 eV. This demonstrates the ability of tuning the energy gaps of the nanocomposites efficiently. The energy gaps are inversely related to the wavelengths of absorption. Therefore, tuning the energy gaps can alter the absorption into longer or shorter wavelengths. According to that, all nanocomposites are expected to show a redshift in the absorption wavelengths towards the infrared (IR) region. In comparison, carboxylation caused a slight reduction of 0.015–0.105 eV in the energy gaps of all configurations except the last configuration (B5-CG-C) where the gap increased by about 0.022 eV.

Boron is a trivalent atom which can form three bonds with carbon and leave one hole (vacancy) on the fourth site of carbon. Doping with boron creates holes as charge carriers in the GQD lattice, which is known as p-type doping. In p-type doping, new unoccupied energy states are created within the energy gap above the HOMOs; hence, the energy gap is reduced [50]. These new energy states accept electrons from the HOMO level and leave holes behind; thus, the number of created energy states is equal the number of created holes. Fig. 5 shows the density of states (DOS) of centre and edge doping with boron. As illustrated, boron created one new unoccupied energy state above the HOMO. The new energy state of centre doping (B1-UG-C) is higher than the HOMO whereas the new energy state in edge doping (B2-UG-C) overlaps with the HOMO. This makes the energy gap of centre doping (1.114 eV and 1.119 eV) narrower than edge doping (2.488 eV and 2.474 eV). According to that, central doping might give larger red-shift in the UV–Vis absorption spectrum.

Optical properties play a vital role in enhancing the DSSCs' efficiency. Absorption affects the generation of current which is directly related to the efficiency. Photosensitizers must have strong absorption in the visible region (400–800 nm) since it is in the solar radiation range. Therefore, they are capable of absorbing photons which excite their electrons. Excitation of electrons is essential to initiate the current generation. These excited electrons are injected into the conduction band (CB) of the photoanode and start to flow as a current. Table 1 represents the maximum absorption wavelengths of all systems using CPCM-B3LYP/6-31 + G (d,p) in methanol solvent. The same data is represented as a graph of absorbance versus wavelength in Fig. 6 . Catechin exhibits a maximum absorption at 210.96 nm in the UV region (100–300 nm) which is comparable with experimental data (278 nm) [35,36] with only 64 nm difference. Hybridization of catechin with GQD shifted the absorption to longer wavelengths (redshift) from the UV region to the visible region at 454.24 nm; thus, UG-C can perform as a photosensitizer. The redshift is attributed to the energy gap reduction of catechin, see Table 1. Doping UG-C with central boron atom (B1-UG-C) shifted the absorption from 451.44 nm to 477.06 nm (redshift), while edge doping with boron (B2-UG-C) caused an insignificant redshift of absorption to 455.36 nm. B1-UG-C exhibits longer wavelengths than B2-UG-C as expected from FMOs and DOS diagrams. Among the three decorated UG-C with boron, only B4-UG-C caused a redshift (488.91 nm) while both B3-UG-C and B5-UG-C caused a blue shift. Carboxylation shifted the absorption of all nanocomposites to longer wavelengths. Among all the studied nanocomposites, B3-CG-C and B4-CG-C exhibit the longest absorption wavelengths of 502.08 nm and 504.17 nm, respectively. However, all nanocomposites exhibit absorption in the visible region unlike catechin. This confirms the viability of using them as photosensitizers.

Oscillator strength is proportional to the square of the transition dipole moment which is responsible for the transition to occur [51]. Higher oscillator strength intensity promotes the probability of the transition. It is also related to the LHE which increases as the oscillator strength increases. Enhancing the LHE means enhancing the ability of capturing photons, this can contribute to produce more current. As can be seen in Table 1, UG-C shows two transitions at 454.24 and 451.44 nm with oscillator strengths of 1.59 and 1.24, respectively. Both transitions are more intense than the catechin transition. This enhancement raised the LHE from 0.538 to 0.974 and 0.942; hence, UG-C is a better capturer of photons. In comparison to UG-C, the absorption intensity decreased to 0.776 and 0.717 of B1-UG-C and B2-UG-C, respectively, which also decreased LHE. All the three decorated UG-C show less intense absorption than the undecorated UG-C. Their LHE also decreased to the range of 0.630–0.690. Carboxylation has a mild effect on the intensity. It only increased and decreased the intensity with no more than 0.2 strength. The intensity of all carboxylated systems increased except CG-C and B4-CG-C since it decreased, the same trend can be noticed in the LHE values. UG-C has the most intense absorption and the highest LHE (0.974). However, the LHE of most nanocomposites is close to unity making them suitable photon capturers.

The iso-density shows the molecular charge distribution of the contributed orbitals of each transition. As shown in Table 1, the charge density of the more intense transition of UG-C is localized on the GQD entity. This raises the probability of the recombination of electrons. The less intense transition shows that the charge density of the HOMO is localized on catechin, whereas the charge density of the LUMO +1 is localized on the GQD. The charge distribution on different parts of the molecule leads to intramolecular charge transfer (ICT). As a result, transferring the charge between different parts inhibits the electron hole recombination of electrons and enhances the electron injection. Reducing the recombination contributes to produce more current. Besides the UG-C, eight nanocomposites show an ICT character. They are CG-C, B2-UG-C, B2-CG-C, B3-UG-C, B3-CG-C, B4-UG-C, B4-CG-C, and B5-UG-C. According to that, they might enhance the current production in a DSSC.

The maximum emission wavelengths, excitation energy, oscillator strength, excited state lifetime, and short circuit current density are tabulated in Table 2 , while the emission spectra graph of all nanocomposites are illustrated in Fig. 7 . The maximum emission wavelengths of all nanocomposites (except B4-UG-C and B4-CG-C) are shifted to longer wavelengths regarding their absorption maxima. Only B4- UG-C and B4-CG-C are shifted to shorter wavelengths. The difference between the absorption and emission peaks is referred to as Stokes shift. Larger Stokes shift indicates stronger ICT character and larger difference between the geometries of ground and excited states [52,53]. The Stokes shift ranges from 44.42 nm (UG-C) to 102.29 nm (B5-CG-C). Doping and decorating with boron are responsible for achieving larger Stoke shifts of 45.92 nm (B1-UG-C) to 100.67 nm (B5-UG-C). It is noticed that Stokes shift has been further increased upon carboxylation of all nanocomposites except B2-CG-C. This means carboxylation contributes to shift the emission peaks to longer wavelengths.

Longer excited state lifetime increases the opportunity for excited electrons to be injected from the dye to the TiO2; thus, electron-hole recombination is inhibited [42]. As seen in Table 2, central and edge doping increased the excited state lifetime in comparison to UG-C. Central doping has longer lifetimes than edge doping. All decorated nanocomposites with boron groups lengthened the excited state lifetime even longer than central doping. Carboxylation increased the excited state lifetime of all nanocomposites by 0.262–1.618 ns. This enhancement in lifetime length can lead to better electron transfer ability.

Short-circuit current density (JSC) is directly related to power conversion efficiency according to Eq. (6). This means generating more current can enhance the power conversion efficiency of DSSCs. Table 2 shows that all nanocomposites generate short-circuit densities in the range from 6.18 mAcm−2 to 15.28 mAcm−2. As can be noted the B1-CG-C system has the highest short-circuit current density of 15.28 mAcm−2; this may be due to its higher photon flux. In comparison to B1-CG-C, the central and edge doping with boron show a less ability to generate the current of 14.48 mAcm−2 and 10.81 mAcm−2, respectively. According to that, central doping with boron (B1-UG-C) might obtain higher power conversion efficiency than edge doping (B2-UG-C). However, decorating with boron further decreased the current generation to 7.56 mAcm−2 (B4-UG-C), 11.56 mAcm−2 (B4-UG-C), and 6.18 mAcm−2 (B5-UG-C). On the other hand, carboxylation increased the current generation significantly in all systems except CG-C/UG-C and B4-CG-C/B4-CG-C which have almost a negligible effect. The difference in short-circuit current density values between uncarboxylated and carboxylated systems take place as: 0.41, 0.5, 2.2, 4.82, 0.34, 5.77 mAcm−2 for UG-C/CG-C, B1-UG-C/B1-CG-C, B2-UG-C/B2-CG-C, B3-UG-C/B3-CG-C, B4-UG-C/B4-CG-C, and B5-UG-C/B5-CG-C, respectively. To sum up, carboxylated systems exhibit better current generation than uncarboxylated systems. As well, among all the uncarboxylated/carboxylated systems B1-CG-C has the highest generation of current; thus, it can be predicted to have the highest power conversion efficiency among them and is assumed to be the most promising nanocomposite.

A proper alignment of energy levels is required to inject electrons from the LUMO of the photosensitizer to the CB of TiO2 as previously illustrated in Fig. 4. Efficient electron injection can be achieved by considering the electrochemical parameters in Table 3 and Fig. 8
. The table represents the ground state oxidation potential (GSOP)
E
O X
dye
, the excited state oxidation potential (ESOP)
E
O X
dye *
, the injection and regeneration free energies
Δ G
i n j
and
Δ G
r e g
, respectively. The change in free energy indicates the spontaneity of a process. A negative sign of the change in free energy means the process is thermodynamically favourable and spontaneous. Therefore, negative
Δ G
i n j
indicates that the ESOP of a photosensitizer is above the CB of TiO2. More negativities indicate higher spontaneity. Central doping of UG-C with boron decreased the free injection energy slightly (see Fig. 8). While edge doping and decoration with boron increased the free injection energy to −2.184 eV (B3-UG-C). Carboxylation decreased the free energy of injection for all nanocomposites except B3-CG-C. However, all nanocomposites exhibit negative
Δ G
i n j
values less than −1.482 eV which is less than the minimal required driving force (0.2 eV) [54]. This insures high efficiency of electron injection of all nanocomposites.

Dye regeneration is essential to restore the oxidized photosensitizer which is the final step in a DSSC mechanism. This supports the sustainability and maintains the stability of the DSSC. Efficient dye regeneration from the electrolyte potential to the HOMO of the photosensitizer can be expressed by the free energy of regeneration. Small or negative
Δ G
r e g
indicates that the GSOP is lower than the redox potential of the electrolyte. This also means that the regeneration of the dye is thermodynamically favourable and spontaneous. UG-C shows the best regeneration ability with −1.522 eV driving force. Doping and decoration of UG-C with boron decreased the
Δ G
r e g
to −0.135 eV (B4-UG-C) as a minimum. Carboxylation improved the dye regeneration as it decreased the free energy of regeneration of all nanocomposites except of the CG-C. Six out of twelve nanocomposites (CG-C, B1-UG-C, B1-CG-C, B2-CG-C, B5-UG-C, and B5-CG-C) are above the minimal driving force point (0.4 eV) [54] while the remaining nanocomposites are below it.

The open-circuit voltage VOC is the maximum voltage at zero current density. It is known as the potential difference between the LUMO of the photosensitizer and the CB of TiO2. This potential difference induces an electrical force which makes the free electrons flow thus more current is generated. Therefore, higher VOC means more electrical force is induced increasing the DSSC efficiency. It is directly related to the overall energy conversion efficiency of a DSSC and evidently depends on the energy level of LUMO of the photosensitizers. The VOC values of all nanocomposites are similar with a range of 0.704 eV. Positive voltage indicates that electrons can be transferred from the LUMO of the photosensitizer to the CB of the TiO2 easily. Edge doping with boron (B2-UG-C) and decoration with borinic group (B4-UG-C) increased the voltage of UG-C. Carboxylation stabilized the LUMOs of all nanocomposites. As a result, the VOC decreased.

The reorganization energies of electron, hole, hole-electron difference, and total in methanol solvent are listed in Table SD 3. According to Marcus theory, the rate constant is inversely proportional to reorganization energy. For the efficient charge transport process, low reorganization energy is necessary, which facilitates electron injection and hence improves solar power conversion efficiency in a DSSC. Electron and hole reorganization energies are illustrated in Fig. 9 . Catechin has the highest value of λ hole which approves the improvement of hole mobility in all nanocomposites. It also has the highest λ elec among all systems, except B3-UG-C, indicating improvement of electron mobility. Hybridization of catechin with uncarboxylated graphene decreased the hole and electron reorganization energies by 0.325 eV and 0.346 eV respectively. A similar effect is achieved by hybridization with carboxylated graphene (CG-C).

Doping with central boron (B1-UG-C) enhanced both hole and electron mobility to 0.056 eV and 0.063 eV. Inducing a trivalent atom among four carbons creates a new hole; thus, hole mobility increases as noticed in B1-UG-C case. Edge doping with boron showed similar mobilities to the UG-C and CG-C. Central and edge doping with boron have higher hole mobilities than decorated systems with boron. However, all decorated systems show improvement in hole mobility in comparison to isolated catechin. All designed nanocomposites, except B3-UG-C, exhibit better electron mobility than catechin.

Carboxylation improved both λ hole and λ elec in CG-C comparing to UG-C; however, it did not further improve the dopped and decorated systems except B3-UG-C. All nanocomposites (except B3-CG-C, B4-UG-C, and B4-CG-C) show smaller λ hole values compared to the typical λ hole value (0.290 eV) of N,N′-diphenyl-N,N0-bis (3-methylphenyl)-(1,10-biphenyl)-4,40-diamine (TPD) [55]. All nanocomposites (except B3-UG-C) show smaller λ elec values compared to the typical λ elec value (0.276 eV) of tris(8-hydroxyquinolinato) aluminium (III) (Alq3 [55]). As seen in Fig. 9, the electron mobilities of UG-C, CG-C, both central dopped systems, both edge dopped systems, and B3-UG-C are lower than their hole mobilities. This makes them good electron carriers. While all the decorated systems with boron (except B3-UG-C) have higher hole mobility indicating better hole transportation ability.

The hole-electron difference indicates a balanced transportation rate of holes and electrons which improves the luminescence efficiency in DSSCs [56]. More balanced transportation rates lead to higher short-circuit current density values. B1-UG-C and B1-CG-C show the best-balanced transportation rates with a Δλ of 0.008 eV and 0.007 eV, respectively. Carboxylated systems (except B2-CG-C and B5-CG-C) have more balanced transportation rates (see Table SD 3). This makes them more promising in increasing JSC values and improving the DSSCs' efficiency.

The ability to donate and accept electrons of the dye materials relates to ionization potential (IP) and electron affinity (EA), respectively. Several molecular description parameters including adiabatic/vertical IPa/v, EAa/v, hardness (ha/v), and hole/electron extraction potential (EPhole/elec) are tabulated in Table 4 .

The value of IP needs to be small to allow an electron injection to the semiconductor and hole formation in the dye molecule. Central and edge doping with boron increased the IP in comparison to UG-C. Decoration with boron groups achieved better IP values than doping. Carboxylation increased the IP values which decreased their electron donation ability; hence, uncarboxylated nanocomposites are better electron injectors. Among all nanocomposites, B5-UG-C has the best IP (4.154 eV). However, all designed nanocomposites have lower IP values than catechin IP (5.950 eV) which makes their electron donation and injection to the CB easier than catechin.

The high EA value will enable efficient electron access into the LUMO level of the dye. Central and edge doping with boron have higher and lower EA values than UC-C, respectively. The decorated nanocomposites with boron have higher EA values than edge doping and lower EA values than central doping. Carboxylation increased the EA of all systems which enhances their electron acceptance from the RP. B1-CG-C is the best electron acceptor among all nanocomposites. All designed nanocomposites have better EAs than the catechin EA (1.083 eV); hence, they are more capable of accepting and regenerating electrons form the RP to the photosensitizer.

Chemical hardness indicates the resistance towards molecule polarization upon small deformation. Soft molecules are easier polarizable than hard ones due to less excitation energy required; thus, lower chemical hardness enables higher charge transfer ability which is expected to favour a larger conversion efficiency of a DSSC. The insertion of boron atom/groups softened the UC-C skeleton. The decorated nanocomposites are softer and harder than edge and central doping, respectively. Carboxylation softened all nanocomposites providing higher flexibility. Among all nanocomposites, B1-CG-C is the softest nanocomposite with a very low hardness value near to 0 eV. All designed nanocomposites are softer than catechin. As a result, they are polarizable and might exhibit better charge transfer performance and generate more JSC.

RIP and REA are the relative electron donation and acceptance, respectively. Fig. 10 illustrates the full electron donor–acceptor map (FEDAM) which shows the electron donation and acceptance ability of nanocomposites. It is divided into four quadrants. First I, second II, third III, and fourth IV quadrants represent the good acceptor-bad donor, bad acceptor-bad donor, bad acceptor-good donor, and good acceptor-good donor, respectively. Catechin is in the second quadrant which indicates poor electron donation and acceptance since it is an insulator. UG-C and CG-C give better matching with the reference IP and EA values, but they are still bad acceptors and bad conductors. B4-CG-C is in the first quadrant which refers to good acceptance ability. B1-UG-C has higher IP and EA values than the reference values; hence it has the best acceptance and conducting properties. All other nanocomposites are located in the third quadrant indicating that all of them are good conductors.

Noncovalent interactions (NCI) can control the chemical processes. These interactions include strong and weak attraction such as hydrogen bonding, van der Waals, dipole-dipole, and London dispersion. Also, they represent the steric repulsion. Johnson et al. developed a new approach to analyse these NCI depending on the electron density and its first and second derivatives [57]. A two-dimensional plot of reduced density gradient (RDG) versus the sign of the second Hessian eigenvalue (λ 2) multiplied by electron density is used to reveal the NCI in this method. RDG is calculated from the following equation:
Eq. 20
R D G =

3
π 2

1 3
|
∇ ρ
|
ρ
4 3
where ρ is the electron density and
|
∇ ρ
|
is it electron density gradient. NCI can be visualized in real space by NCI-RDG analysis. Fig. 11 represents the 2D/3D NCI-RDG scatter maps of UG-C and CG-C nanocomposites at CPCM/B3LYP/6-31G+(d,p) in methanol. The interactions strength is distinguished by a colour code. Strong attractive hydrogen bonding (blue), weak attractive van der Waals (green), and strong steric repulsion (red). The type of NCI is determined by the sign of λ 2 in the Laplacian of density ∇2ρ equation:
Eq. 21 ∇2ρ = λ 1 + λ 2 + λ 3
where λ 1, λ 2, and λ 3 are the first second and third electron density Hessian matrix eigenvalues. Bonding (λ 2 < 0) and non-bonding (λ 2 > 0) interactions can be distinguished from each other by the sign of the λ 2. For weak van der Waals λ 2 ≈ 0. This is illustrated in the colour code at the beginning of Fig. 11. The interaction between the catechin compound and nanocomposites is illustrated as a green-orange disk in all cases. This indicates the weakly repulsive interaction between C--C and C--N atoms and weakly attractive interaction between H--O and H--H atoms. For edge carboxylation, the same manner is observed. Weakly repulsive interaction between C--O atoms and weakly attractive interaction between the H–O atoms. The scatter maps of all nanocomposites at the same level of theory are illustrated in Fig. SD 1.

Intramolecular charge transfer (ICT) gives an important indication of inhibiting the electron-hole recombination and increasing the current generation (R). According to Le Bahers's, three main indices can be studied to predict the ICT process [58]. They are charge transfer distance (DCT) in Å, transferred charges quantity (qCT) in au, and tCT index. ICT can be enhanced by increasing both the charge transfer distance (DCT) and quantity (qCT). Besides that, lowering tCT index indicates smaller orbitals overlap of the electron-donating and electron-accepting regions which contributes to enhance the ICT character [46]. The Le Bahers's ICT parameters of isolated catechin and all nanocomposites are tabulated in Table 5 .

The charge transfer distance (DCT) is the spatial distance between the electron density decreasing and increasing regions of the barycenters [58]. Longer distance provides better charge separation. As seen in Table 5, hybridization of catechin with uncarboxylated GQD (UG-C) doubled the DCT to 1.359 Å. This means the charges of UG-C are better separated than those of catechin. Central and edge doping with boron (B1-UG-C and B2-UG-C) both have larger DCT than catechin but shorter than UG-C. Two configurations of the uncarboxylated decorated systems with boron groups (B4-UG-C and B5-UG-C) increased the DCT to 2.096 Å and 1.813 Å, respectively. This provides better separation of the electron density decreasing and increasing regions than UG-C. B4-UG-C has the maximum DCT (2.096 Å) among all nanocomposites. Carboxylation decreased the DCT of all nanocomposites. However, all nanocomposites, except B2-CG-C and B5-CG-C, have longer DCT than isolated catechin which may improve the ICT character of most nanocomposites.

Transferred charges quantity (qCT) is calculated from the integration of the electron density decreasing and increasing regions of the barycenters [58]. Better ICT character is observed with higher qCT. Hybridization of catechin with uncarboxylated GQD (UG-C) decreased the qCT slightly by 0.01 au which has no significant effect. Central doping with boron (B1-UG-C) almost has the same value of qCT of catechin with only 0.002 au increase. All the other uncarboxylated systems (BX-UG-X, where X = 2,4,5) have lower qCT than catechin within a small difference that does not exceed 0.274 au. Carboxylation increased the qCT of all nanocomposites to higher values than catechin. B5-CG-C has highest qCT value (0.992 au) among all the carboxylated systems. The qCT of all carboxylated nanocomposites is in the range from 0.977 au to 0.992 au. However, all the designed nanocomposites have a close charge quantity value to catechin within a small range of 0.299 au; thus, they are supposed to exhibit better ICT character than isolated catechin.

The value of tCT index should be less than 1.6 Å to prove the feasibility of the functional in describing the energy of transition [58]. All the obtained values are much less than 1.6 Å down to −7.881 Å (B1-UG-C). The designed nanocomposites have better tCT index values than catechin decreasing the overlap of donating and accepting regions. All these negative values assume that the designed nanocomposites support ICT process which improves the electron injection.

Electron density difference maps (EDDMs) show the charge separation in a molecule which is essential for effective charge transfer between the ground state and the first excited state. Fig. 12 illustrates the EDDMs of UG-C, CG-C, and B3-UG-C. Both electron density depletion (blue) and electron density increment (purple) of UG-C are localized on GQD with no obvious charge separation. Carboxylation in CG-C has no significant effect on the electron density difference distribution. The addition of boron atom/group has localized the electron density on boron which can induce some charge defect in the nanocomposite lattice. A huge localization of electron density depletion and increment on borane group in B3-UG-C is observed. The other doped and decorated systems with boron are represented in Fig. SD 2.

The behaviour of organic photosensitizers can be explained by studying the nonlinear optical (NLO) properties [7]. Delocalisation of ICT is used to determine the NLO parameters, such as static dipole moment (μ) in Debye, mean polarizability (α0) in esu, and first order hyperpolarizability (β0) in esu. According to that, NLO parameters can describe the ICT efficiency which affects the short-circuit current density; thus, the energy conversion efficiency of the DSSC is affected [7,46]. Better NLO properties enhance the performance of photosensitizers since they are directly related to each other [46]. The three abovementioned NLO parameters (μ, α0, β0) of p-nitroaniline as a reference, isolated catechin, and all nanocomposites are tabulated in Table 6 .

As shown in Table 6, the static dipole moment (μ) of catechin is 45% worse than p-nitroaniline which is a typical NLO compound. μ significantly increased by 5.9 Debye through hybridization with GQD (UG-C). Further increase of μ values is obtained via central doping and decoration with boron compounds of the UG-C nanocomposite (BX-UG-C, where X = 1,3,4,5). Among all systems, B4-UG-C gave the highest μ values that is 30% better (higher) than p-nitroaniline. Carboxylation of all nanocomposites decreased the μ values. From Table 6, all nanocomposites show a large enhancement of static dipole moment (μ) in comparison to isolated catechin; hence, improvement of DSSC conversion efficiency is expected.

Larger mean polarizability (α0) value has been obtained from UG-C (949 esu) than catechin (260 esu) (see Table 6). Central and edge doping (B1-UG-C and B2-UG-C) increased the α0 values by 41 and 16 esu, respectively. Among the decorated systems, only B5-UG-C has higher α0 value (955) than UG-C. The mean polarizability (α0) values of all uncarboxylated nanocomposites vary in the range from 936 esu to 990 which indicate better NLO properties than isolated catechin. Carboxylation increased the mean polarizability (α0) values of all nanocomposites. This trend is the opposite of the static dipole moment (μ) trend. B1-CG-C shows the highest α0 value (1015). The increase of α0 values is due to the induced polarity of the electron withdrawing carboxyl groups as seen previously in the MEP maps in Fig. 2. All nanocomposites have higher α0 than p-nitroaniline resulting in much better NLO properties.

The first order hyperpolarizability (β0) of catechin (1414 esu) almost doubly increased to 2592 esu in UG-C. Further enhancement was obtained through central and edge doping with boron. B1-UG-C shows the highest β0 value among all nanocomposites. Among the uncarboxylated decorated systems, only B5-UG-C increased the β0 value to 8644 esu in comparison with UG-C nanocomposite. Carboxylation increased the β0 values in edge doping and all the three decorated systems with boron up to 10748 esu (B5-CG-C). Simultaneously, CG-C and B1-CG-C have lower β0 values than their uncarboxylated forms. However, all nanocomposites have higher μ, α0, β0 values than isolated catechin which confirms the enhancement of NLO properties and lead to larger short-circuit current density and better DSSC conversion efficiency.

In this DFT/TD-DFT study, several parameters were calculated theoretically in order to evaluate the performance of catechin and its nanocomposites as photosensitizers in DSSCs. The effect of hybridizing GQD with catechin, doping/decorating with boron atom/group, and carboxylation were studied. All the designed nanocomposites are stable according to the positive values of binding energies. All LUMOs and HOMOs of nanocomposites are appropriately aligned with the CB and RP, respectively. The LUMO of B5-CG-C and HOMO of B4-UG-C are the closest energy levels to the CB and RP, respectively making them the most promising electron transferring nanocomposites. The energy gap of catechin was significantly decreased to match the visible region wavelengths. This shifted the absorption of catechin (210.96 eV) from the UV region to the visible region in the range 416.56 eV–504.17 eV. Carboxylation shifted all the absorption peaks to longer wavelengths and increased the intensity of most of them. According to that, most of carboxylated nanocomposites have higher LHE than uncarboxylated systems. This can provide higher current production.

Excited state lifetime increased in the following trend edge doped with boron < central doped with boron < decorated with boron < and carboxylated. B4-CG-C has the longest excited state lifetime of 4.563 ns. Also, carboxylation exhibits better current generation than uncarboxylated systems. B1-CG-C generated the highest JSC of 15.28 mAcm−2. Higher current generation would increase the power conversion efficiency of a DSSC.

All nanocomposites exhibit negative Δ
G inj
values less than the minimal required driving force. Six out of twelve nanocomposites are above the minimal driving force point. The VOC values of all nanocomposites are ranging slightly with less than 1 eV variation. Stabilization of LUMOs of carboxylated nanocomposites decreased the VOC values. All nanocomposites have sufficient electron injection and exhibit reasonable voltage.
Catechin has the highest value of λ hole which means all the designed nanocomposites are better hole carriers. Also, all nanocomposites, except B3-UG-C, are better electron carriers than catechin.

All nanocomposites have lower IP values than catechin which enhances their electron injection ability. Uncarboxylated systems are better injectors than carboxylated ones. B5-UG-C has the best IP (4.154 eV) among all nanocomposites. Central doping and carboxylation improved the EA of the photosensitizer. B1-CG-C has the highest EA value in this study. All the designed nanocomposites are better electron acceptors than catechin. The hardness decreased upon insertion of boron atom/groups and carboxylation. B1-CG-C is the softest one with almost 0 eV hardness. All nanocomposites are more polarizable and have better abilities of electron acceptance and donation.

All nanocomposites, except B2-CG-C and B5-CG-C, have longer DCT than isolated catechin which enhances the ICT character. Uncarboxylated nanocomposites have better separation of charge than carboxylated ones. B4-UG-C has the best separation distance of 2.096 Å. All nanocomposites have close qCT values to catechin within a small range of 0.299 au. All nanocomposites have negative tCT index values lower than the catechin resulting in increasing the ICT and electron injection processes. All the designed nanocomposites have enhanced NLO properties in comparison to isolated catechin with improved μ, α0, β0 values that may lead to larger JSC.
This study confirms that nanocomposites of hybridized catechin with different GQDs that are doped/decorated with boron atom/group and/or carboxylated can be promising photosensitizers in DSSCs.
Authors confirm that the data supporting this study's findings are available within the article and its supplementary data.

Nuha Alsmani conceptualized, designed, computed, analyzed, and interpreted these data, and drafted/wrote the manuscript related to this research as part of her master's thesis. Prof. Nuha Wazzan has been a supervisor of this study, provided guidance on the concept, design, and draft development, and approved the final version of the manuscript. Dr. Ohoud S. Al-Qurashi is a collaborator and author of the present work. All Authors have participated in (a) conception and design or analysis and interpretation of the data, (b) drafting the article or revising it critically for important intellectual content and (c) approval of the final version.
